tests.test_body:
declare void @foo(i32)

define void @main(i1 %c1, i1 %c2, i32 %x) {
; CHECK-LABEL: @main(
; CHECK-NEXT:  entry:
; CHECK-NEXT:    br i1 [[C1:%.*]], label [[L:%.*]], label [[END:%.*]]
; CHECK:       L:
; CHECK-NEXT:    [[XOR:%.*]] = xor i32 [[X:%.*]], -1
; CHECK-NEXT:    [[NEG:%.*]] = xor i32 [[XOR]], -1
; CHECK-NEXT:    call void @foo(i32 [[NEG]])
; CHECK-NEXT:    br label [[L]]
; CHECK:       end:
; CHECK-NEXT:    ret void
entry:
  br i1 %c1, label %L, label %end

L:                                                ; preds = %L, %entry
  %d.1 = phi i8 [ undef, %entry ], [ -1, %L ]
  %conv = sext i8 %d.1 to i32
  %xor = xor i32 %x, %conv
  %neg = xor i32 %xor, -1
  call void @foo(i32 %neg)
  br label %L

end:                                              ; preds = %entry
  ret void
}
tests.source_program:
declare void @foo(i32)

define void @main(i1 %c1, i1 %c2, i32 %x) {
entry:
  br i1 %c1, label %L, label %end

L:                                                ; preds = %L, %entry
  %d.1 = phi i8 [ undef, %entry ], [ -1, %L ]
  %conv = sext i8 %d.1 to i32
  %xor = xor i32 %x, %conv
  %neg = xor i32 %xor, -1
  call void @foo(i32 %neg)
  br label %L

end:                                              ; preds = %entry
  ret void
}

tests.test_body:
@a = external global i64

define void @fn1(i1 %bc) {
; CHECK-LABEL: define void @fn1(
; CHECK-SAME: i1 [[BC:%.*]]) {
; CHECK-NEXT:  entry:
; CHECK-NEXT:    br label [[FOR_COND:%.*]]
; CHECK:       for.cond:
; CHECK-NEXT:    [[LV:%.*]] = load i32, ptr @a, align 4
; CHECK-NEXT:    [[BF_CLEAR:%.*]] = and i32 [[LV]], -131072
; CHECK-NEXT:    [[BF_SET:%.*]] = or i32 1, [[BF_CLEAR]]
; CHECK-NEXT:    [[BF_CLEAR_1:%.*]] = and i32 [[BF_SET]], -131072
; CHECK-NEXT:    [[BF_SET_1:%.*]] = or i32 1, [[BF_CLEAR_1]]
; CHECK-NEXT:    br i1 [[BC]], label [[FOR_COND]], label [[EXIT:%.*]]
; CHECK:       exit:
; CHECK-NEXT:    store i32 [[BF_SET_1]], ptr @a, align 4
; CHECK-NEXT:    ret void
entry:
  br label %for.cond

for.cond:                                         ; preds = %for.cond, %entry
  %tmp = phi i1 [ undef, %entry ], [ true, %for.cond ]
  %conv = zext i1 %tmp to i32
  %lv = load i32, ptr @a, align 4
  %bf.clear = and i32 %lv, -131072
  %bf.set = or i32 %conv, %bf.clear
  %bf.clear.1 = and i32 %bf.set, -131072
  %bf.set.1 = or i32 1, %bf.clear.1
  br i1 %bc, label %for.cond, label %exit

exit:                                             ; preds = %for.cond
  store i32 %bf.set.1, ptr @a, align 4
  ret void
}
tests.source_program:
@a = external global i64

define void @fn1(i1 %bc) {
entry:
  br label %for.cond

for.cond:                                         ; preds = %for.cond, %entry
  %tmp = phi i1 [ undef, %entry ], [ true, %for.cond ]
  %conv = zext i1 %tmp to i32
  %lv = load i32, ptr @a, align 4
  %bf.clear = and i32 %lv, -131072
  %bf.set = or i32 %conv, %bf.clear
  %bf.clear.1 = and i32 %bf.set, -131072
  %bf.set.1 = or i32 1, %bf.clear.1
  br i1 %bc, label %for.cond, label %exit

exit:                                             ; preds = %for.cond
  store i32 %bf.set.1, ptr @a, align 4
  ret void
}

issue.title: [NewGVN] PHI nodes with undef incoming values cause problems
issue.body:
|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Bugzilla Link | [36335](https://llvm.org/bz36335) |
| Version | trunk |
| OS | All |
| Blocks | llvm/llvm-project#30343  |
| CC | @efriedma-quic,@fhahn,@nunoplopes |

Tested with trunk revision 324806.

issue.author: zhendongsu

comments.author: fhahn
comments.body:
> Because we don't reuse congruence classes, and variables only get processed once 
> per iteration,  the way to do this off the top of my head:

> Outside the iteration loop create DenseMap<CC, int> where the int is the count 
> of members in the class.

> Fill it in at the end of the first iteration.
> At the end of each subsequent iteration, compare to the cc member counts. If the > number goes down or stays the same, that's oaky.
> If the number goes up, assert, because something became more equivalent.

> This will not catch all cases, because it could be that something swapped with 
> something else. But it should be quick and catch a bunch of cases for you.

> The way to catch all cases would be Densemap<CC, copy of member list>.
> Fill it in at the end of the first iteration.
> At the end of each iteration, if the size of the class is =, compare them and 
> assert if not equal.
> If it's greater, assert.
> If it's less, that's fine.

Great, thanks again! I'll look into this once I have a bit more time.

comments.author: fhahn
comments.body:
> I think in this particular case, we end up with values wrongly marked as congruent,
> because we move %xor to the correct class in the second iteration, but then use the 
> existing,invalid leader (%neg) when simplifying  `%neg = xor i32 %xor, -1`, which 
> unfortunately simplifies to %xor; %neg stays in the congruence class incorrectly and > we stop

> If you are saying we use a non-class-leader when simplifying, that is
> definitely a bug.
> Things should only be replaced, at a given point in time, with things believed
> in the same class.

> If you are saying the leader does not become correct until the end of the
> iteration, that is working as intended, and simplification needs to be able to
> deal with it.

Sorry for not being clearer, I meant we use a leader is not correct in the second iteration. When simplifying this leader (the opportunity to correct it), we lookup and use the incorrect leader and it ends up staying in the wrong class, because we simplify it to an expression in the same CC. I realize this still might not be the best best description, but I am not sure how to put it in a better way.


> We actually do have other cases without undef where variables start out 
> equivalent and become *more* equivalent and more constant, like that.  I don't > believe i ever produced one that explicitly makes them constant, but we
> definitely have non-equivalent variables that later become equivalent

Thanks! I'll see if I can get such an example and get back once I have more info where things are going wrong, hopefully.

comments.author: fhahn
comments.body:
For the small IR snippet posted earlier, I don't think there is a
problem with conflicting assumptions in InstSimplify, but rather with
what operands we ask it to simplify (I disabled backtracking for
InstSimplify, to make sure).

> We also completely propagate the congruence state, it should never end up with conflicting assumptions in play when it is done 

I think in this particular case, we end up with values wrongly marked as congruent, because we move %xor to the correct class in the second iteration, but then use the existing,invalid leader (%neg) when simplifying  `%neg = xor i32 %xor, -1`, which unfortunately simplifies to %xor; %neg stays in the congruence class incorrectly and we stop

IIUC this is only an issue with undefs in PHIs, because currently we allow the value to be used as undef for simplifications and then later we change it to a constant (which might be different to the constants the simplifications assumed for the undef). In the (similar) case of PHIs with constant incoming values, we are correcting the false assumptions, because we assume it is constant first, but once the second incoming constant becomes feasible, we assume it is a variable and we update the CC info that assumed it was a constant.

I assume in the paper they assume there are no undefined values similar to LLVM's undef.

comments.author: fhahn
comments.body: *** Bug llvm/llvm-bugzilla-archive#35173 has been marked as a duplicate of this bug. ***

comments.author: fhahn
comments.body:
I reduced this to the code below


declare void @​foo(i32)

define void @​main(i1 %c1, i1 %c2, i32 %x) {
entry:
  br i1 %c1, label %L, label %end

L:
  %d.1 = phi i8 [ undef, %entry ], [ -1, %L ]
  %conv = sext i8 %d.1 to i32
  %xor = xor i32 %x, %conv
  %neg = xor i32 %xor, -1
  call void @​foo(i32 %neg)
  br label %L

end:
  ret void
}

bin/opt -newgvn will generate


define void @​main(i1 %c1, i1 %c2, i32 %x) {
entry:
  br i1 %c1, label %L, label %end

L:                                                ; preds = %L, %entry
  %xor = xor i32 %x, -1
  call void @​foo(i32 %xor)
  br label %L

end:                                              ; preds = %entry
  ret void
}



We  replaced %neg with %xor = xor i32 %x, -1, which is not correct I think.



I think the underlying problem is how we handle phi nodes with undef values. In the code below, in the first iteration, only the incoming undef value is live, and we use ConstantExpression(undef) for it. I

n the second iteration, the -1 incoming value becomes live, and we use ConstantExpression(-1) for the PHI node. But I am not sure that is correct.

If we use the undef for any simplification in the first iteration, we (implicitly) assume it takes a concrete constant value (e.g. sext i8 undef to i32 == 0 assumes undef takes value 0 IIUC) and we discover congruence info based on that assumption. Now if we use ConstantExpression(-1) for the phi in the second iteration, we do simplifications based on the assumption phi == -1, but we might also use congruence info based on the assumption phi == 0, which leads to incorrect results.


Here is what happens for the test case, in more detail:

1. we visit the phi node and mark %d.1 as undef.
2. we simplify %conv to 0 assuming %d.1 becomes 0.
3. we simplify %xor  to %x (xor i32 %x, 0)
4. we simplify %neg to xor %x -1 (%xor == %x from previous step)

For expression (xor, %x, -1) we have a congruence class with leader %neg = xor i32 %xor, -1

5. mark edge L->L as executable, and we visit the phi node again, and we now know its value must be -1.
6. we simplify conv to -1
7. For %xor, we now get expression (xor, %x, -1) and we place it in the same CC as %new
8. the leader for %xor is %new, so we try to simplify (xor (xor %xor, -1), -1) to %xor

We later replace %neg with %xor.
